topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on the screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into the otherwise mundane scroll of the afternoon. *For Sale: 14 Willow Creek Lane.* My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake that seemed to steal all the air from my lungs. I blinked again, scrubbing at my eyes as if the sight would somehow dissolve. Willow Creek Lane. That was where the chipped paint on the porch swing used to look like a fortress against the world. My hands started to tremble, the fine motor control dissolving into a clumsy, useless shaking. I stood up so fast my chair scraped loudly across the hardwood floor. I needed to check the listing again, to confirm it wasn't some elaborate prank, but my fingers felt thick and unresponsive against the keyboard. A strange, hot pressure built behind my sternum, making my chest feel tight, like a balloon about to burst. I paced the small living room three times, my footsteps echoing in the sudden, profound silence. What if it was real? What if the ghost of that old house was actually being offered up for acquisition?

[story 2]

I stared at the screen until the pixels swam before my eyes. The words—'Sold,' 'Asking Price,' 'Property Details'—were mocking me. My jaw locked so tightly I could feel the muscles in my cheeks strain. A sudden, dizzying wave washed over me, making the room tilt slightly. I dropped the phone onto the sofa cushion as if it were suddenly radioactive. I began to pace the perimeter of the apartment, my movements jerky and uncoordinated. Every instinct screamed at me to call someone, to run, but my feet seemed rooted to the spot. My heart hammered against my ribs with such violence that I could hear it thumping over the sound of my own ragged breathing. I pressed the heels of my palms against my temples, trying to physically contain the churning sensation inside. It felt like the foundations of my composure were crumbling, revealing a terrifying, hollow space underneath. I couldn't focus on anything; the world had narrowed down to that single, impossible address.

[story 3]

When the listing loaded, the air in the room seemed to thin out. I leaned in close, my nose almost brushing the glass, trying to absorb the details—the photos of the overgrown garden, the faded blue shutters. A strange, bubbling energy started in my core, a frantic, internal fizzing that demanded release. I started whispering, my voice coming out reedy and high-pitched, barely audible. "No. No, this isn't right." I spun around, catching my reflection in the darkened television screen. My face was pale, slick with a sudden cold sweat despite the stifling warmth of the room. My hands flew up, gripping the arms of the chair until my knuckles were white and aching. I started pacing again, faster this time, a desperate, caged animal needing movement. The sheer impossibility of it—that place, *my* place, now available—sent a violent tremor through my entire body, leaving me shaky and utterly unable to settle down.
